metadata.name: rpy2-rinterface
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU General Public License v2 or later (GPLv2+)
metadata.summary: Low-level interface from Python to the R.

modules.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.bufferprotocol
documentation: Python Buffer Protocol for R arrays.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.bufferprotocol.getrank
documentation:
Get the rank (number of dimensions) of an R array.

The R NULL will return a rank of 1.
\:param cdata: C data from cffi
\:return: The rank

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.bufferprotocol.getshape
documentation:
Get the shape (size for each dimension) of an R array.

The rank of the array can optionally by passed. Note that is potentially
an unsafe operation if the value for the rank in incorrect. It may
result in a segfault.

```
param cdata

C data from cffi

return

A Tuple with the sizes. The length of the tuple is the rank.
```

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.bufferprotocol.getstrides
documentation:
Get the strides (offsets in memory when walking along dimension)
for an R array.

The shape (see method `getshape`) and itemsize must be specified.
Incorrect values are potentially unsage and result in a segfault.

```
param cdata

C data from cffi.

param shape

The shape of the array.

param itemsize

The size of (C sizeof) each item in the array.

return

A tuple with the strides. The length of the tuple is rank-1.
```

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.embedded_mswin._initr_win32
documentation:
Initialize the embedded R.

```
param interactive

Should R run in interactive or non-interactive mode?
```

if `None` the value in `_DEFAULT_R_INTERACTIVE` will be used.
\:param \_want_setcallbacks: Should custom rpy2 callbacks for R frontends
be set?.
\:param \_c_stack_limit: Limit for the C Stack.
if `None` the value in `_DEFAULT_C_STACK_LIMIT` will be used.

modules.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.callbacks
documentation:
Callbacks available from R's C-API.

The callbacks make available in R's C-API can be specified as
Python functions, with the module providing the adapter code
that makes it possible.

documentation: Return the count for the protection stack.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.memorymanagement.ProtectionTracker.protect
documentation:
Pass-through function that adds the R object to the short-term
stack of objects protected from garbase collection.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.memorymanagement.ProtectionTracker.unprotect
documentation: Release the n objects last added to the protection stack.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.memorymanagement.ProtectionTracker.unprotect_all
documentation:
Release the total count of objects this instance knows to be
protected from the protection stack.

Convenience class to keep track of R's C-level protection stack.

Mixing this with direct calls to Rf_protect() and Rf_unprotect() in
the C API from Python, or even using Rf_protect() and Rf_unprotect(),
is strongly discouraged.
TODO: make it extend ContextManager and delete the function
rmemory ?

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib._rinterface_capi.findvar_in_frame
documentation:
Safer wrapper around Rf_findVarInFrame()

Run the function Rf_findVarInFrame() in R's C-API through
R_ToplevelExec().

Note: All arguments, and the object returned, are C-level
R objects.

Args:
- rho: An R environment.
- symbol: An R symbol (as returned by Rf_install())
Returns:
The object found.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib._rinterface_capi._python_index_to_c
documentation:
Compute the C value for a Python-style index.

The function will translate a Python-style index that
can be either positive or negative, if the later to
indicate that indexing is relative to the end of the
sequence, into a strictly positive or null index as
use in C.

Raises an exception IndexError if out of bounds.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpEnvironment

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpEnvironment.find
documentation:
Find an item, starting with this R environment.

Raises a `KeyError` if the key cannot be found.

This method is called `find` because it is somewhat different
from the method {meth}`get` in Python mappings such {class}`dict`.
This is looking for a key across enclosing environments, returning
the first key found.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpEnvironment.frame
documentation: Get the parent frame of the environment.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpEnvironment.enclos
documentation: Get or set the enclosing environment.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpEnvironment.keys
documentation: Generator over the keys (symbols) in the environment.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpEnvironment.__iter__
documentation: See method `keys()`.

Proxy for an R "environment" object.

An R "environment" object can be thought of as a mix of a
mapping (like a `dict`) and a scope. To make it more "Pythonic",
both aspects are kept separate and the method `__getitem__` will
get an item as it would for a Python `dict` while the method `find`
will get an item as if it was a scope.

As soon as R is initialized the following main environments become
available to the user:
- `globalenv`: The "workspace" for the current R process. This can
  be thought of as when `__name__ == '__main__'` in Python.
- `baseenv`: The namespace of R's "base" package.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpVectorAbstract.from_iterable
documentation: Create an R vector/array from an iterable.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpVectorAbstract.from_memoryview
documentation:
Create an R vector/array from a memoryview.

The memoryview must be contiguous, and the C representation
for the vector must be compatible between R and Python. If
not the case, a {class}`ValueError` exception with will be
raised.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpVectorAbstract.from_object
documentation:
Create an R vector/array from a Python object, if possible.

An exception {class}`ValueError` will be raised if not possible.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface_lib.sexp.SexpVector

Base abstract class for R vector objects.

R vector objects are, at the C level, essentially C arrays wrapped in
the general structure for R objects.

modules.name: rpy2.situation
documentation:
This module is currently primarily intended to be used as a script.
It will print information about the rpy2's environment (Python version,
R version, rpy2 version, etc...).

functions.name: rpy2.situation.get_r_home
documentation:
Get R's home directory (aka R_HOME).

If an environment variable R_HOME is found it is returned,
and if none is found it is trying to get it from an R executable
in the PATH. On Windows, a third last attempt is made by trying
to obtain R_HOME from the registry. If all attempt are unfruitful,
None is returned.


documentation:
Get the parsed output of calling 'R CMD CONFIG <about>'.

Returns a tuple (parsed_args, unknown_args), with parsed_args
having the attribute `l`, 'L', and 'I'.

methods.name: rpy2.rlike.container.OrdDict.reverse
documentation: Reverse the order of the elements in-place (no copy).

Implements the Ordered Dict API defined in PEP 372.
When `odict` becomes part of collections, this class
should inherit from it rather than from `dict`.

This class differs a little from the Ordered Dict
proposed in PEP 372 by the fact that:
not all elements have to be named. None as a key value means
an absence of name for the element.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface.local_context
documentation:
Local context for the evaluation of R code.

Args:
- env: an environment to use as a context. If not specified (None, the
  default), a child environment to the current context is created.
- use_rlock: whether to use a threading lock (see the documentation about
  "rlock". The default is True.

Returns:
Yield the environment (passed to env, or created).

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface.vector
documentation:
Create an R vector.

While the different types of R vectors all have their own class,
the creation of array in Python is often available through factory
function that accept the type of the array as a parameters. This
function is providing a similar functionality for R vectors.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface.initr_simple
documentation: Initialize R's embedded C library.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface.initr_checkenv
documentation:
Initialize the embedded R.

```
param interactive

Should R run in interactive or non-interactive mode?
```

if `None` the value in `_DEFAULT_R_INTERACTIVE` will be used.
\:param \_want_setcallbacks: Should custom rpy2 callbacks for R frontends
be set?.
\:param \_c_stack_limit: Limit for the C Stack.
if `None` the value in `_DEFAULT_C_STACK_LIMIT` will be used.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface._getrenvvars
documentation: Get the environment variables defined by the R front-end script.
TODO: This function could be used by situation.py. May be better to

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface.rternalize
documentation:
Make a Python function callable from R.

Takes an arbitrary Python function and wrap it in such a way that
it can be called from the R side.

This factory can be used as a decorator, and has an optional
named argument "signature" that can be True or False (default is
False). When True, the R function wrapping the Python one will
have a matching signature or a close one, as detailed below.

The Python ellipsis arguments\`\*args\` and `**kwargs` are
translated to the R ellipsis `...`.

For example:

```python @rternalize(signature=True) def foo(x, *args, y=2):     pass

```

will be visible in R with the signature:

```r function (x, ..., y)

```

The only limitation is that whenever `*args` and `**kwargs` are
both present in the Python declaration they must be consecutive.
For example:

```python def foo(x, *args, y=2, **kwargs):     pass

```

is a valid Python declaration. However, it cannot be "rternalized"
because the R ellipsis can only appear at most once in the signature
of a given function. Trying to apply the decorator `rternalize` would
raise an exception.

The following Python function can be "rternalized":

```python def foo(x, *args, **kwargs):     pass

```

It is visible to R with the signature

```r function (x, ...)

```

Python function definitions can allow the optional naming of required
arguments. The mapping of signatures between Python and R is then
quasi-indentical since R does it for unnamed arguments. The check
that all arguments are present is still performed on the Python side.

Example:

```python @rternalize(signature=True) def foo(x, *, y, z):     print(f'x: {x[0]}, y: {y[0]}, z: {z[0]}')     return ri.NULL

```

```pycon
>>> _ = foo(1, 2, 3)
x: 1, y: 2, z: 3
ValueErro: None
>>> _ = foo(1)
TypeError: rternalized foo() missing 2 required keyword-only arguments: 'y' and 'z'
>>> _ = foo(1, z=2, y=3)
x: 1, y: 3, z: 2
>>> _ = foo(1, z=2, y=3)
x: 1, y: 3, z: 2
```

Note that whenever the Python function has an ellipsis (either `*args`
or `**kwargs`) earlier parameters in the signature that are
positional-or-keyword are considered to be positional arguments in a
function call.

```
param function

A Python callable object. This is a positional
```

argument with a default value `None` to allow the decorator function
without parentheses when optional argument is not wanted.

```
return

A wrapped R object that can be use like any other rpy2
```

object.

functions.name: rpy2.rinterface.process_revents
documentation:
Process R events.

Calling this function a regular interval can help ensure that
R is processing input events (e.g., resizing of a window with
graphics).

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpSymbol
documentation: An unevaluated R symbol.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface._MissingArgType

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface._MissingArgType.__bool__
documentation: This is always False.

Missing argument in a call to an R function.

When evaluating a function, arguments that are not specified
can take a default value when named, otherwise they will
take a special value that indicates that they are missing.

documentation:
"Evalute the R "promise".

```
param env

The environment in which to evaluate the
promise.
```

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpVectorWithNumpyInterface

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpVectorWithNumpyInterface.__array_interface__
documentation:
Return an `__array_interface__` version 3.

Note that the pointer returned in the items 'data' corresponds to
a memory area under R's memory management and that it will become
invalid once the area once R frees the object. It is safer to keep
the rpy2 object proxying the R object alive for the duration the
pointer is used in Python / numpy.

Numpy-specific API for accessing the content of a numpy array.

This interface implements version 3 of Numpy's `__array_interface__`
and is only available / possible for some of the R vectors.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.ByteSexpVector
documentation:
Array of bytes.

This is the R equivalent to a Python {class}`bytesarray`.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.BoolSexpVector
documentation:
Array of booleans.

Note that R is internally storing booleans as integers to
allow an additional "NA" value to represent missingness.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.ListSexpVector
documentation:
R list.

An R list an R vector (array) that is similar to a Python list in
the sense that items in the list can be of any type, whereas most
other R vectors are homogeneous (all items are of the same type).

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.PairlistSexpVector
documentation:
R pairlist.

A R pairlist is rarely used outside of R's internal libraries and
a relatively small number of use cases. It is essentially a LISP-like
list of (name, value) pairs.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.LangSexpVector

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface.LangSexpVector.from_string
documentation:
Create an R language object from a string.

This creates an unevaluated R language object.

Args:
    s: R source code in a string.

Returns:
    An instance of the class the method is called from.

An R language object.

To create from a (Python) string containing R code
use the classmethod `from_string`.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpClosure.rcall
documentation:
Call/evaluate an R function.

Args:
- keyvals: a sequence of key/value (name/parameter) pairs. A
  name/parameter that is None will indicated an unnamed parameter.
  Like in R, keys/names do not have to be unique, partial matching
  can be used, and named/unnamed parameters can occur at any position
  in the sequence.
- environment: an optional R environment to evaluate the function.

methods.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpClosure.closureenv
documentation: Closure of the R function.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpS4
documentation:
R "S4" object.

S4 objects as one of the available forms of Object-Oriented Programming
in R.

classes.name: rpy2.rinterface.SexpExtPtr
documentation: R "External Pointer" object.
